_comment: Deterministic pronunciation overrides for the TTS pipeline (doc_to_speech + doc_to_podcast) — the PRIMARY text-level pronunciation lever (the old LLM normalize/respell pass was removed: it mangled long-but-ordinary words espeak already says right). Kokoro/espeak pronounces most multi-syllable words correctly on its own, so keep this file for the EXCEPTIONS only: acronyms said as letters ('ER' -> 'E R', 'UX' -> 'U X'), numbers said as digits ('911' -> 'nine one one'), and the rare botched word/name. Keys are matched case-insensitively as whole words OR phrases and replaced in the text BEFORE synthesis; values are respellings that Kokoro's espeak phonemizer pronounces the way we want. Applied LONGEST-KEY-FIRST, so a multi-word phrase key wins over a bare word — this is how context-dependent homographs are handled: never override a bare homograph (it has more than one correct sound), override the disambiguating PHRASE instead. Worked example, 'live': espeak already says inflected verbs right ('lives'/'lived'/'living' -> /lIv/) and defaults bare 'live' to /laIv/ (LYVE); it also gets some phrases right ('live in') but not others ('live with', 'i live here'). For a context it gets wrong, add a phrase key like 'live with' -> 'liv with' (respelling 'liv' -> /lIv/, 'lyve' -> /laIv/; do NOT use 'lighve', which inserts a stray g). This is the text-level lever; for surgical IPA control see Lever 3 (kokoro-onnx create(is_phonemes=True)). Validate a new entry by comparing phonemes: cd services/tts && uv run python -c "from kokoro_onnx.tokenizer import Tokenizer as T; t=T(); print('want', t.phonemize('<respelling>')); print('base', t.phonemize('<word>'))" — a good override changes only the target vowel/stress, leaving the rest of the phoneme string identical.
